chosen_title: Millions take omega-3 fish oil for brain health but a new study found no benefit
label: HEALTH
headline: Fish Oil Won't Save Your Brain
image_prompt: A single translucent amber fish oil gel capsule suspended in midair at eye level, its swirling golden interior clearly visible through the soft shell. The setting is a minimalist morning kitchen counter slightly out of focus — a half-empty supplement bottle, a glass of water, and a crumpled weekly pill organizer in the background, suggesting years of quiet daily habit. The capsule's curved surface catches a bright accent glow from the upper left, making it gleam like a small lantern against the cool blue-grey shadows. The lower third of the frame is a calm, dark counter surface.
tweets: Millions of people take fish oil every day specifically to protect their brain. A major new clinical study just found it doesn't work — not even a little. 🧵

Omega-3 fish oil is one of the world's best-selling supplements. The pitch: DHA, a key omega-3 fatty acid, physically builds brain cell membranes — so supplementing it should mean better memory and lower Alzheimer's risk. That logic spread everywhere.

The new study reviewed the clinical trial evidence directly. Researchers tracked whether DHA supplements actually improved memory or slowed cognitive decline. Result: no measurable benefit — not for memory, not for Alzheimer's risk, even with long-term supplementation.

The theory had solid biology behind it: DHA makes up roughly 30% of the brain's fatty acids, and Alzheimer's patients often show lower levels. But low DHA may be a symptom of neurodegeneration — not a cause. Topping it up externally doesn't reverse or prevent the damage.

What this likely means for your supplement shelf:
• Omega-3 for 'brain health' appears to be money wasted — clinical evidence isn't there
• Heart health evidence is stronger, though still debated among researchers
• No supplement has yet been proven to prevent Alzheimer's in hea…

Most popular supplements run years ahead of the trial data that would actually prove them. This one just got caught up.
